content.text: Based on the official Claude Cookbook documentation and the Claude API course,
"Routing"
is a
multi-LLM workflow pattern that dynamically selects specialized processing paths based on input characteristics
, similar to how a router directs internet traffic to the right server.
What It Is
Routing takes the user's original input and feeds it into a
routing step
(usually a call to Claude) that
categorizes the task
, then forwards the input to a
particular follow-up processing pipeline
specialized for that task type.
The Basic Flow
User Input → Router (Claude categorizes) → Route Decision → Specialized Pipeline → Final Output
Step
What Happens
Routing step
Claude categorizes user input/task into predefined categories
Route decision
Based on classification, select which pipeline to use
Specialized pipeline
Forward to customized prompt/tools/workflow for that category
Final output
Processed result from the specialized pipeline
How It Works
The routing pattern follows a clear sequence:
Prepare user request
— Send original input to the router
Router classifies
— Claude instance with strict system prompt categorizes the intent
Select pipeline
— Based on classification, route to specific workflow
Execute specialized pipeline
— Each route has its own custom prompt, tools, and workflow
Return result
— Final output from the specialized pipeline
Example: Video Script Generator

:
- User asks: "Python functions"
- Router categorizes: →
Educational

(vs. Entertainment, Documentary, etc.)
- Route to: Educational video pipeline
- Pipeline writes: Script with clear explanations, thought-provoking examples, educational tone
- Output: Educational video script about Python functions
Semantic Routing (Advanced)
Modern routing uses
semantic embeddings
to match task intent to agent capabilities:
Phase
What Happens
Agent Registration
Plugin generates semantic embeddings from agent capability descriptions using Claude Opus
Task Intake
Natural language task wrapped with context: codebase files, conversation history, tool access
Intent Embedding
Router sends task description to Claude Opus for semantic embedding generation (500ms-1s)
Capability Matching
Compute cosine similarity between task embedding and agent capability embeddings (~100ms)
Load Balancing
Adjust scores: penalize overloaded agents, prioritize available ones (~50ms)
Route Decision
Dispatch to highest-scoring available agent with task context (~50ms)
Result Verification
Evaluate output relevance; if poor match, trigger re-routing (1-2 sec)
Key metric
: Semantic routing improves first-attempt match rates to
When to Use It
Routing is ideal when:
Condition
Why Routing Helps
Different task types need different approaches
Each category gets specialized handling
Intents vary widely across inputs
Router determines which path to take
Need specialized tools/prompts per category
Each route has custom configuration
Want to optimize cost/performance
Route simple tasks to cheaper models, complex to powerful ones
Multi-agent system with specialists
Route to agent with highest domain expertise
Routing vs. Prompt Chaining vs. Parallelization
The three basic multi-LLM workflow patterns:
Pattern
How It Works
When to Use
Prompt Chaining
Sequential subtasks, each builds on previous
Task needs step-by-step transformation
Parallelization
Independent subtasks across multiple LLMs concurrently
Subtasks don't depend on each other
Routing
Dynamically selects specialized path based on input
Different task types need different approaches
State-Based Routing (Advanced)
Routing can also be
state-based
, where the next agent/action depends on project state:

# routing.yaml example
Mode: NOUVEAU (no .plan/ directory)
  Trigger: First run on new project
  Next Agent: instructor
  Action: Ask discovery questions

Mode: EN_COURS (recent .plan/, modified <7 days)
  Trigger: Resuming active project
  Next Agent: Determined by state.json.current_phase
  Action: Continue from where left off

Mode: REPRISE (old .plan/, modified >7 days)
  Trigger: Resuming abandoned project
  Next Agent: instructor
  Action: Audit architecture, check for obsolescence
Phase transitions

discovery (completed) → architecture phase → architect
architecture (completed) → check gate → user_approval → ✓ Approved → implementation → executor
implementation (completed) → validation → writor
validation (completed) → check gate → user_approval → ✓ Approved → complete
Mental Model
Think of routing like a
hospital triage system

: patients arrive, a nurse (router) assesses their condition and categorizes them, then routes them to the appropriate specialist (cardiologist, orthopedist, neurologist) who has the right tools and expertise for that specific problem.
Key Takeaways
Routing dynamically selects specialized LLM paths based on input characteristics
Takes user input → classifies it → routes to specialized pipeline
Router is usually a Claude instance with strict system prompt for categorization
Each route has its own custom prompt, tools, and workflow
Semantic routing uses embeddings to match task intent to agent capabilities (85%+ match rate)
Can be state-based: route depends on project state, phase, blockers, gates
One of three basic multi-LLM patterns: Prompt Chaining, Parallelization, Routing
Useful for: video generators, multi-agent systems, task classifiers, content pipelines
Trades cost/latency for improved performance on varying task types
Routing is the orchestration mechanism that directs diverse inputs to the right specialized processors, enabling Claude to handle varied task types efficiently.
How It Works in Practice
Understanding the internals of routing helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Best Practices
Production experience with routing has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Performance Benchmarks and Cost Analysis
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Security Considerations
Deploying routing in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.
Advanced Best Practices
Production experience with routing has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Advanced Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Performance Benchmarks and Cost Analysis
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Security Considerations
Deploying routing in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.
Best Practices
Following best practices ensures effective use of Routing in real-world applications.
Start with the simplest approach that solves your problem before adding complexity.
Test edge cases thoroughly, especially boundary conditions and error states.
Document your implementation decisions for future maintainability.
Monitor performance metrics and optimize based on actual usage patterns.
Follow security best practices and validate all inputs at system boundaries.
Use version control and code reviews for all production changes.
Establish clear error handling strategies before deployment.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Understanding typical pitfalls in Routing helps you avoid costly errors.
Over-engineering solutions when simpler approaches would suffice — start minimal.
Neglecting error handling, leading to silent failures in production.
Skipping testing because the implementation appears straightforward.
Ignoring rate limits and quotas when designing at scale.
Not considering the user experience impact of latency or error states.
Performance and Optimization
Optimizing Routing for production environments requires attention to key performance factors.
Factor
Impact
Optimization Strategy
Latency
High
Use caching and streaming responses
Throughput
Medium
Implement request batching and connection pooling
Cost
High
Monitor token usage and optimize prompt length
Reliability
Critical
Add retries with exponential backoff
Security Considerations
Security is paramount when implementing Routing in production systems.
Never expose API keys in client-side code or public repositories.
Validate and sanitize all user inputs before processing.
Implement proper authentication and authorization controls.
Audit logs for all sensitive operations and data access.
Follow the principle of least privilege for all service accounts.
Summary

This section covered Routing in depth, including core concepts, practical implementation approaches, and production considerations. Apply these patterns consistently to build robust, maintainable systems.
What the Routing Pattern Solves

The routing pattern addresses a fundamental challenge in LLM-powered applications: different categories of input require meaningfully different processing, and sending all inputs through a single, monolithic handler produces worse results than specialized handlers for each category. A customer support system might receive billing questions, technical bugs, feature requests, and abuse reports — each needing different context, different response style, and different downstream actions. A routing layer classifies each incoming request and directs it to the handler best equipped to serve it. The benefit compounds: specialized handlers can have tighter system prompts with domain-specific knowledge, routed to cheaper or faster models when appropriate, and even to non-LLM handlers (a regex match for an account lookup, for example). Routing is the first optimization you should apply when you notice that a general-purpose agent produces inconsistent quality across different input types.
The core insight: a specialized handler outperforms a general handler on its target input category, at the cost of needing a router to direct traffic.
Routing reduces context window waste: specialized handlers need only the context relevant to their category, not every possible scenario.
Enables model tier optimization: simple categories route to fast/cheap models; complex categories route to powerful/expensive models.
Non-LLM routing targets are valid: deterministic handlers (database lookups, regex extractors) are often faster and cheaper for clear-cut categories.
Failure mode prevention: a single general agent may try to handle out-of-scope requests; a router can identify and reject them explicitly.
Composability: routers can be nested — a top-level router splits by domain, domain routers split by intent within that domain.
Classifier-Based Routing with an LLM

LLM-based classification routes inputs by asking a model to assign a category label before any downstream processing begins. The classifier model receives the input and a list of valid categories with descriptions, and returns a single category label (or a ranked list for ambiguous cases). This approach generalizes to any input type without requiring hand-crafted rules and handles novel phrasings gracefully, but introduces an extra API call with its associated latency and cost. To minimize this overhead, use the fastest and cheapest model available for classification — claude-haiku-4-5 or equivalent. The classifier prompt should include explicit category definitions with two or three examples per category to reduce misclassification on boundary cases. Return a structured JSON response from the classifier (e.g., {"category": "billing", "confidence": 0.95}) to make confidence-gated fallback logic straightforward.
Use the cheapest available model for classification — the classifier prompt is simple and does not require deep reasoning.
Define categories with descriptions and 2-3 examples per category to reduce boundary-case misclassification.
Request a confidence score alongside the category label to enable fallback handling for uncertain classifications.
Return JSON from the classifier: {"category": "...", "confidence": 0.0-1.0} for easy programmatic parsing.
For N categories, consider whether N < 10 — above that, consider hierarchical routing (first route to domain, then to intent).
Log misclassifications from user feedback to build a labeled dataset for evaluating and improving the classifier prompt.
Rule-Based and Hybrid Routing

Rule-based routing uses deterministic conditions — keyword matching, regex patterns, structural features of the input, or metadata — to assign categories without any LLM call. This is the fastest and cheapest routing approach and should be the first choice for categories with clear, unambiguous signals. Examples: a message containing "cancel my subscription" nearly always routes to the cancellation handler; an input that is entirely numeric routes to the calculation handler; a request that arrives via a specific API endpoint routes to the corresponding handler regardless of content. Hybrid routing combines rule-based fast paths with LLM-based fallback for ambiguous cases: apply rules first, and only invoke the classifier model when no rule fires. This dramatically reduces the fraction of requests that require an LLM classification call, often to under 20%, while maintaining high accuracy for the ambiguous minority.
Rule-based routing: keyword lists, regex patterns, input length, metadata fields, or source channel — zero LLM cost, instant classification.
Ordered rule evaluation: evaluate rules from most specific to least specific; the first matching rule wins.
Hybrid routing: apply all rules first; if no rule fires, invoke the LLM classifier on the unmatched input.
A typical hybrid router reduces LLM classification calls to 10-30% of total traffic, dramatically cutting latency and cost.
For API-driven systems, route based on the endpoint path or request headers before examining the request body.
Cache classifier results for identical inputs — the same question asked twice should not invoke two LLM calls.
Routing to Different Models and Handlers

One of the most powerful applications of routing is model tier selection: routing simple, well-defined tasks to small, fast, cheap models and complex, ambiguous tasks to larger, more capable models. For example, a factual lookup that requires retrieving a stored value can route to claude-haiku-4-5 with a 200-token budget; a multi-step reasoning task that requires synthesizing multiple sources routes to claude-sonnet-4-5 or claude-opus-4-5 with a 4000-token budget. This produces a cost reduction proportional to the fraction of traffic that qualifies for cheaper model handling, which in practice is often 60-80% of requests. Routing can also direct traffic to entirely non-LLM handlers: a database query, a cached response, a deterministic calculation, or a rule-based response generator. These handlers respond in milliseconds and cost nothing per token.
Model tier routing: classify task complexity and route to haiku (simple), sonnet (moderate), or opus (complex).
Token budget routing: simple tasks get max_tokens=256; complex tasks get max_tokens=4096 — avoids paying for unused capacity.
Non-LLM routing targets: database lookups, cache hits, static response templates, regex-based calculators.
A/B testing via routing: route a percentage of traffic to an experimental handler to measure quality improvements.
Latency routing: time-sensitive requests route to faster models; batch-processed requests route to higher-quality models.
Cost logging: track which route each request takes to measure cost distribution and identify optimization opportunities.
